seo.title: Kamphaeng Phet Province, Thailand — Complete Guide 2026
seo.metaDescription: Everything about Kamphaeng Phet Province: UNESCO World Heritage ruins, Khlong Lan National Park, Ping River, and famous kluai khai bananas. Area: 8,607 km², population: 700,862. Plan your visit.

overview: Kamphaeng Phet — meaning 'Diamond Wall' in Thai — is a historically rich province in the upper central plains, straddling the Ping River roughly 360 km north of Bangkok. Its ancient walled city and forest temples form part of the UNESCO World Heritage Site shared with Sukhothai and Si Satchanalai, making it one of Thailand's most significant archaeological landscapes. Beyond the ruins, the province is blanketed by fertile agricultural land famous for kluai khai (egg bananas) and chakungrao grass jelly, while Khlong Lan and Mae Wong national parks offer waterfalls, forest trails, and wildlife in the western highlands.
geography: Kamphaeng Phet covers 8,607 km² in the upper central region of Thailand, bounded by the Ping River to the east and rising into mountainous forested terrain towards Tak Province in the west. The landscape transitions from flat alluvial plains ideal for rice and sugarcane cultivation to dense montane forests. The province lies at the convergence of the central plains and the lower northern uplands, giving it a diverse ecology and a historically strategic location along ancient trade routes.
highlights: Kamphaeng Phet Historical Park — UNESCO World Heritage Site with laterite ruins of the Sukhothai-era walled city and forest temple zone
Khlong Lan National Park — dramatic 100-meter Khlong Lan Waterfall, jungle trekking, and wildlife including elephants and gibbons
Mae Wong National Park — misty mountain summit of Mokju, exceptional birdwatching, and pristine forest cover
Kluai Khai bananas — the province's iconic egg-shaped aromatic banana, celebrated at a September harvest festival
Kamphaeng Phet National Museum — two buildings of Sukhothai-era Buddha images, prehistoric artifacts, and excavated temple objects
Ping River — scenic riverside promenade through the old city with local markets and evening food stalls
Phra Ruang Hot Springs — natural thermal pools at 45–65°C with affordable entry, ideal after a day of ruin-hopping
Chakungrao grass jelly — a local specialty dessert unique to Kamphaeng Phet, sold fresh at markets throughout the province

bestTimeToVisit.peak: Nov–Feb: Cool and dry season with temperatures of 18–30°C — the most comfortable period for exploring the open-air historical park ruins and hiking in Khlong Lan National Park. Clearest skies and lowest humidity.
bestTimeToVisit.shoulder: Mar–May: Hot season with temperatures climbing to 35–40°C. The historical park ruins are exposed and very hot midday; visit early morning. March–April is still dry so waterfall flows are reduced at Khlong Lan.
bestTimeToVisit.lowSeason: Jun–Oct: Monsoon season brings heavy rains and lush green scenery. Khlong Lan Waterfall is at full spectacular force. Roads to national parks can become muddy. Hotel prices drop significantly.
bestTimeToVisit.recommendation: Visit November to February for the best combination of comfortable temperatures, dry weather, and ideal conditions for exploring the UNESCO ruins. If Khlong Lan Waterfall is your priority, visit August–October during peak flow.

topAttractions.name: Kamphaeng Phet Historical Park
topAttractions.description: A UNESCO World Heritage Site (jointly listed with Sukhothai and Si Satchanalai since 1991) encompassing the ruins of a 14th–15th century walled city built during the Sukhothai Kingdom. The park divides into an inner city zone — with Wat Phra Kaew and Wat Phra That behind the ancient laterite walls and moat — and an outer forest temple zone featuring atmospheric ruins of Wat Chang Rob (elephant-base temple), Wat Phra Si Iriyabot (with four Buddha postures), and dozens of laterite chedis emerging from the jungle.
topAttractions.entranceFee: 300 THB foreigners / 30 THB Thais (~$8 USD). Combined ticket available for both zones.

topAttractions.name: Kamphaeng Phet National Museum
topAttractions.description: Two museum buildings adjacent to the Historical Park housing one of Thailand's finest collections of Sukhothai-period Buddhist sculpture, including large bronze and stucco Buddha images in the distinctive Kamphaeng Phet style. Prehistoric artifacts, ceramics, and objects excavated from local temples provide cultural context for the surrounding ruins.
topAttractions.entranceFee: 100 THB foreigners / 20 THB Thais (~$3 USD). Closed Mondays and Tuesdays.

topAttractions.name: Khlong Lan National Park
topAttractions.description: A 300 km² national park in the western highlands of Kamphaeng Phet, dominated by dense dipterocarp and hill evergreen forest. The park's centrepiece is the stunning Khlong Lan Waterfall — a wide 100-meter cascade that thunders into a cool pool ideal for swimming. Wildlife includes Asian elephants, gaurs, gibbons, and over 200 bird species. Trekking trails lead to viewpoints and smaller falls.
topAttractions.entranceFee: 200 THB adults / 100 THB children (~$6 USD). Camping available.

topAttractions.name: Mae Wong National Park
topAttractions.description: A vast 1,008 km² park straddling Kamphaeng Phet and Nakhon Sawan provinces, rising to the mist-shrouded peak of Mokju. The park is renowned among birdwatchers for rare species including Siamese fireback pheasants and hornbills. In winter (Nov–Feb) the summit is frequently blanketed in fog, creating a dramatic highland atmosphere. The park protects one of the largest contiguous forest blocks in central Thailand.

topAttractions.name: Phra Ruang Hot Springs
topAttractions.description: Natural geothermal springs on the outskirts of Kamphaeng Phet city with water temperatures ranging from 45 to 65°C. Visitors can soak in private baths or outdoor pools surrounded by tropical gardens. A relaxing stop after a day exploring the dusty ruins of the historical park. Open daily 8am–6pm.

topAttractions.name: Wat Khu Yang
topAttractions.description: A functioning Buddhist temple notable for its beautifully preserved 19th-century wooden scripture library (Ho Trai) built on poles over a pond — a traditional design intended to protect manuscripts from termites and floods. The carved timber structure is one of the finest surviving examples of traditional northern-central Thai religious architecture in the province.

localFood: Kluai Khai (egg bananas) — Kamphaeng Phet's most famous product: small, almost oval, intensely aromatic bananas grown in the Ping River valley, celebrated at a September festival and sold at markets province-wide

Chakungrao grass jelly — a uniquely local dessert made from dried Platostoma palustre herb, served chilled in sweet syrup; the name 'chakungrao' is specific to Kamphaeng Phet and differs from standard grass jelly elsewhere
Khao Tom Mat — sticky rice stuffed with banana and black beans, wrapped in banana leaves and steamed; a popular roadside snack throughout the province
Nam Prik Num — roasted green chilli dip with charred shallots and garlic, served with blanched vegetables and pork rinds; a northern-central Thai staple found at local restaurants
Gaeng Hang Lay — rich pork belly curry with ginger, turmeric, and tamarind with Burmese-influenced flavours, found at morning markets and local eateries near the old city
Mango with sticky rice — seasonal (Apr–Jun) fresh mango served with warm coconut-cream-drenched sticky rice; particularly good at riverside market stalls in Kamphaeng Phet city
culture: Kamphaeng Phet's cultural identity is shaped by its Sukhothai-period heritage — the ancient walled city was a major garrison town and satellite kingdom of Sukhothai, and the laterite ruins still dominate the urban landscape. The province observes all major Thai Buddhist festivals, with Loy Krathong on the Ping River being particularly atmospheric. The annual Kluai Khai Banana Festival in September draws visitors from across the region with agricultural shows, cultural performances, and market stalls. The local dialect blends central Thai with northern (Lanna) linguistic influences reflecting the province's transitional geography.
gettingThere: From Bangkok, buses depart Mo Chit (Northern Bus Terminal) regularly throughout the day, taking approximately 5 hours and costing 250–350 THB. Win Tour and other operators run frequent departures. There is no direct train service — the nearest railway station is at Phitsanulok (approx. 100 km east), from where buses connect to Kamphaeng Phet in about 1.5 hours. The nearest commercial airports are Phitsanulok Airport (approx. 100 km, Thai AirAsia and Thai Lion Air) and Sukhothai Airport (approx. 140 km, Bangkok Airways). From Sukhothai, buses and minivans make the 70-km run to Kamphaeng Phet in about 1.5 hours for 70 THB. Highway 1 (Phahonyothin Road) connects the province to Bangkok and the north by private vehicle.
gettingAround: The old city ruins and town centre are compact enough to explore by bicycle — rental shops near the historical park charge 50–80 THB per day, making cycling the most popular and practical way to visit the scattered temple zones. Songthaews (shared pickup trucks) run fixed routes between the city and outlying villages. Motorbike taxis and tuk-tuks cover shorter urban trips for 40–80 THB. To reach Khlong Lan or Mae Wong national parks, a private vehicle or chartered songthaew is necessary as public transport does not serve the park gates directly.
travelTips: Visit the Historical Park inner zone at opening (8am) to beat the heat — the outer forest temple zone is shadier and better in mid-morning
A bicycle (50–80 THB/day rental) is the ideal way to move between the inner city zone and the outer Aranayik forest temple zone, about 2 km apart
The National Museum is closed Monday and Tuesday — plan accordingly if combining with the Historical Park
Combine Kamphaeng Phet with Sukhothai (1.5 hours north) or Phitsanulok (2 hours east) for an efficient UNESCO heritage circuit
Khlong Lan Waterfall is at peak flow August–October; outside monsoon season the flow is reduced
The September Banana Festival fills hotels fast — book accommodation a week ahead if visiting during the festival
Respect dress codes at active temples: cover shoulders and knees at Wat Khu Yang and other functioning wats
